frames.narrationSummary: Start with an empty min-heap that will hold only the current top k values.
Scan nums[0] = 5.
The heap has room because size 0 is still below k = 3.
Push 5 into the heap as a new top-k candidate.
Scan nums[1] = 1.
The heap has room because size 1 is still below k = 3.
Push 1 into the heap as a new top-k candidate.
1 is smaller than parent 5, so bubble it up.
Swap heap slots 0 and 1 to restore min-heap order.
Scan nums[2] = 9.
The heap has room because size 2 is still below k = 3.
Push 9 into the heap as a new top-k candidate.
9 is not smaller than parent 1, so the min-heap order already holds.
Scan nums[3] = 3.
The heap is full at size 3, so the new value must beat the root threshold to stay.
3 beats the root threshold 1, so it enters the top-k set.
Replace heap root 1 with 3.
Parent 3 is already below its smaller child 5, so the heap is restored.
Scan nums[4] = 7.
The heap is full at size 3, so the new value must beat the root threshold to stay.
7 beats the root threshold 3, so it enters the top-k set.
Replace heap root 3 with 7.
The smaller child 5 is below parent 7, so sift the replacement down.
Swap parent slot 0 with child slot 1 during sift-down.
Scan nums[5] = 8.
The heap is full at size 3, so the new value must beat the root threshold to stay.
8 beats the root threshold 5, so it enters the top-k set.
Replace heap root 5 with 8.
The smaller child 7 is below parent 8, so sift the replacement down.
Swap parent slot 0 with child slot 1 during sift-down.
Return the top-k values [9, 8, 7] in descending order.
